title: Development
text:
The docs/ directory is a Quarto website project. Pages are authored as .qmd files and rendered to static HTML in docs/_site/.


quarto render docs/
The output is written to docs/_site/. Open docs/_site/index.html in a browser to preview. No pipeline output or R packages are required to render.




Create a .qmd file in docs/ with YAML front matter matching the existing pattern:
---
title: "Page Title"
subtitle: "Short description"
date: last-modified
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
---
Add a navbar entry in docs/_quarto.yml:
- text: "Page Title"
  href: page-title.qmd
Add a bullet to the Reports list in docs/index.qmd.
Render and verify:
quarto render docs/




Configuration lives in docs/_quarto.yml:

title: Development
text:
Publishing to GitHub Pages
The documentation site can be published as a GitHub Pages site for the repository. There are two approaches depending on whether you want to commit the rendered HTML or build it in CI.

Option A: Publish from a Branch (No CI Required)
This approach uses the quarto publish command to push rendered HTML to a dedicated gh-pages branch. No CI configuration is needed.
One-time setup:

Run quarto publish from the project root, targeting GitHub Pages:
quarto publish gh-pages docs/
This renders the site, creates or updates a gh-pages branch containing only the rendered output, and pushes it to the remote.
In the GitHub repository settings, go to Settings > Pages and configure:

Source: Deploy from a branch
Branch: gh-pages, folder / (root)


The site will be available at https://andersenlab.github.io/nemascan-sims-nf/.
Branch note: quarto publish gh-pages works from any branch. It renders the .qmd files in your current working tree and pushes the output to the gh-pages branch, regardless of which branch you are on. This means you can preview-publish docs from a feature branch (e.g. doc-updates) before merging to main.
To update the published site after changing .qmd files:
quarto publish gh-pages docs/
This is a manual step — the site is not automatically rebuilt on push.


Option B: GitHub Actions (Automated Build on Push)
This approach uses a GitHub Actions workflow to render the site on every push to main and deploy the output to GitHub Pages.
One-time setup:

In the GitHub repository settings, go to Settings > Pages and configure:

Source: GitHub Actions

Create .github/workflows/publish-docs.yml:
name: Publish Docs

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - 'docs/**'
      - 'R/**'

permissions:
  contents: read
  pages: write
  id-token: write

concurrency:
  group: "pages"
  cancel-in-progress: false

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: quarto-dev/quarto-actions/setup@v2

      - name: Render site
        run: quarto render docs/

      - uses: actions/upload-pages-artifact@v3
        with:
          path: docs/_site

  deploy:
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment:
      name: github-pages
      url: ${{ steps.deployment.outputs.page_url }}
    steps:
      - id: deployment
        uses: actions/deploy-pages@v4

This workflow triggers only when files in docs/ or R/ change, keeping unrelated pushes fast. The site is published to https://andersenlab.github.io/nemascan-sims-nf/.


Choosing an Approach








Consideration
Option A (quarto publish)
Option B (GitHub Actions)




Setup effort
Minimal
Requires workflow file


Rebuild trigger
Manual command
Automatic on push to main


CI dependency
None
GitHub Actions


Best for
Infrequent doc updates
Docs that change with code

title: Development
text:
Running Tests

Unit Tests
Unit tests use static fixtures and do not require pipeline output:
Rscript tests/run_tests.R


Integration Tests
Integration tests require pipeline output collected by tests/collect_test_data.sh:
# Run pipeline and collect outputs
bash tests/collect_test_data.sh

# Or collect from a previous run
bash tests/collect_test_data.sh --collect-only

# Run integration tests with collected data
TEST_DB_DIR=tests/integration_data/db \
TEST_WORK_DIR=tests/.nf-work \
TEST_EXISTING_ASSESSMENT=tests/integration_data/simulation_assessment_results.tsv \
TEST_DB_ASSESSMENT=tests/integration_data/db_simulation_assessment_results.tsv \
Rscript tests/run_tests.R
Integration test files skip automatically when the required environment variables are not set.

title: Development
text:
Pipeline Stub Runs
Stub runs verify process wiring without executing real computations:
# Fixed architecture
nextflow run main.nf -profile test -stub-run

# Variable architecture
nextflow run main.nf -profile test_variable -stub-run

# With legacy assessment path
nextflow run main.nf -profile test --legacy_assess -stub-run

title: Development
text:
Generating Test Data
The test VCF is not committed to the repository due to its size. Regenerate it from a source CaeNDR VCF:
./data/test/generate_test_vcf.sh /path/to/WI.20220216.hard-filter.isotype.vcf.gz
This produces data/test/test.vcf.gz (~825 MB, 13 strains, chromosomes I/II/V) and its tabix index.

Project Structure
nemascan-sims-nf/
 ├── main.nf                     Pipeline entrypoint
 ├── nextflow.config             Parameters and profiles

 │   ├── db_migration/           DB-path ingestion, analysis, assessment
 │   └── local/                  Helper processes (contig info, eigens)

Each module directory contains a main.nf with the process definition and optionally a resources/ directory with scripts added to $PATH via nextflow.enable.moduleBinaries = true.

title: Assessment Cross-Validation
text:
The nemascan-sims-nf pipeline can produce QTL assessment results through two independent paths:

DB-path (default) — stores GWA results in a Parquet/DuckDB database and performs QTL analysis by querying that database.
Legacy path (opt-in via --legacy_assess) — uses R scripts operating on intermediate files produced during the pipeline run.

Both paths analyze the same GWA output from GCTA_PERFORM_GWA and should produce identical QTL detection, interval boundaries, and significance classifications. The cross-validation analysis quantifies their agreement using three per-replicate concordance scores.

title: Assessment Cross-Validation
text:
When to Use This
Run the cross-validation when:

Validating changes to the DB-path analysis code against the legacy path as a reference
Verifying that a new pipeline deployment produces consistent results
Comparing assessment outputs from different pipeline versions or configurations

title: Assessment Cross-Validation
text:
Concordance Scores
For each mapping entry — a unique combination of simulation replicate (nQTL, simREP, h2, maf, strain_set_id), mapping method, and significance threshold — three scores are computed on a 0-to-1 scale:









Score
What it measures
1 means
0 means




Detection
Detected/Simulated flag agreement per QTL row
All QTL rows agree
None agree


Designation
designate_qtl() outcome category agreement
All designations match
None match


Interval Overlap
Jaccard overlap of [startPOS, endPOS] intervals
Boundaries match perfectly
No overlap

title: Assessment Cross-Validation
text:
Running the Cross-Validation

1. Run the pipeline with both paths
NXF_VER=24.10.4 nextflow run main.nf -profile test,docker --legacy_assess
This produces two assessment TSVs in the output directory:

simulation_assessment_results.tsv (legacy path)
db_simulation_assessment_results.tsv (DB path)



2. Run the automated test suite
The test file tests/testthat/test-assessment_cross_validation.R asserts that all three concordance scores are 1.0 for every mapping entry. It runs as part of the standard test suite:
TEST_EXISTING_ASSESSMENT=/path/to/simulation_assessment_results.tsv \
TEST_DB_ASSESSMENT=/path/to/db_simulation_assessment_results.tsv \
Rscript tests/run_tests.R
If using the tests/collect_test_data.sh helper:
bash tests/collect_test_data.sh
TEST_EXISTING_ASSESSMENT=tests/integration_data/simulation_assessment_results.tsv \
TEST_DB_ASSESSMENT=tests/integration_data/db_simulation_assessment_results.tsv \
Rscript tests/run_tests.R
The tests skip automatically when the environment variables are not set, so unit-only runs are unaffected.


3. Render the interactive notebook
The notebook at tests/notebooks/assessment_cross_validation.qmd produces an HTML report with concordance tables, score distribution histograms, heatmaps, and per-QTL interval detail. To render it after a pipeline run:
quarto render tests/notebooks/assessment_cross_validation.qmd \
  -P existing_assessment:/path/to/simulation_assessment_results.tsv \
  -P db_assessment:/path/to/db_simulation_assessment_results.tsv \
  -P db_dir:/path/to/db
Or set environment variables and render without -P flags:
export TEST_EXISTING_ASSESSMENT=/path/to/simulation_assessment_results.tsv
export TEST_DB_ASSESSMENT=/path/to/db_simulation_assessment_results.tsv
export TEST_DB_DIR=/path/to/db
quarto render tests/notebooks/assessment_cross_validation.qmd
The rendered HTML is written to tests/notebooks/assessment_cross_validation.html.

title: Assessment Cross-Validation
text:
Notebook Contents
The notebook tests/notebooks/assessment_cross_validation.qmd contains:

Data Loading & Overview — reads both assessment TSVs, applies designate_qtl() to classify outcomes, and shows dimension/designation count comparison tables.
Per-Replicate Concordance — computes the three concordance scores for every mapping entry, then presents:

Per-mapping concordance score table
Score distribution histograms
Mean concordance by method and threshold (table + heatmap)
Per-QTL interval Jaccard detail with pass/fail summary

title: Assessment Cross-Validation
text:
Adapting for Other Comparisons
The notebook can be adapted to compare any two assessment TSVs that share the 35-column schema. To compare outputs from different pipeline versions or parameter configurations:

Copy the notebook to a working directory.
Set existing_assessment_path and db_assessment_path to point to the two TSVs being compared (the variable names reflect the original DB-vs-legacy use case but accept any conforming TSV).
Render with Quarto as described above.

The normalize_algorithm_id() helper handles the algorithm_id format difference between DB-path (LMM-EXACT-INBRED_noPCA_EIGEN) and legacy-path (inbred_nopca_EIGEN) by uppercasing and stripping the LMM-EXACT- prefix. If both TSVs use the same format, this normalization is a no-op.

title: nemascan-sims-nf
text: Pipeline documentation for nemascan-sims-nf — a Nextflow pipeline that simulates GWAS for C. elegans and related nematodes, evaluating the power and precision of different mapping algorithms.

title: nemascan-sims-nf
text:
Reports

Marker Set Generation — Phase 1 process documentation covering VCF subsetting, chromosome renaming, LD pruning, and genotype matrix creation.
Trait Simulation — Phase 2 process documentation covering causal variant selection, GCTA phenotype simulation, and PLINK fileset preparation.
GWAS Mapping — Phase 3 process documentation covering GRM construction, phenotypic variance checking, and association mapping under four mode/type conditions.
QTL Detection & Assessment — Phase 4 process documentation covering database ingestion, QTL interval detection, and performance assessment through both the DB and legacy paths.
Database Structure — Parquet database schema reference for marker sets, simulated trait mappings, and metadata tables.
Assessment Cross-Validation — How to run and interpret the cross-validation analysis comparing DB-path and legacy-path assessment outputs.
Development — Contributing guide covering documentation authoring, GitHub Pages publishing, testing, and project structure.

title: Phase 4: QTL Detection & Assessment
text:
The final phase of the pipeline takes GWA results from Phase 3, detects QTL intervals, and assesses detection performance against the simulated causal variants. Two parallel paths produce equivalent output:

DB path (default) — writes results to a Parquet database and queries via DuckDB
Legacy path (opt-in via --legacy_assess) — operates directly on intermediate files using R and GenomicRanges

GWA results (from Phase 3)
 │
 ├─── DB PATH (default) ──────────────────────────────────────────┐
 │                                                                │
 │  Stage 1: Database Ingestion                                   │
 │    DB_MIGRATION_WRITE_MARKER_SET  (barrier)                    │
 │    DB_MIGRATION_WRITE_GWA_TO_DB                                │
 │    DB_MIGRATION_AGGREGATE_METADATA (barrier)                   │
 │                                                                │
 │  Stage 2: QTL Analysis                                         │
 │    DB_MIGRATION_ANALYZE_QTL ──► *_qtl_regions.tsv              │
 │    DB_MIGRATION_ASSESS_SIMS ──► *_db_assessment.tsv            │
 │              ▼                                                 │
 │    db_simulation_assessment_results.tsv                         │
 │                                                                │
 ├─── LEGACY PATH (--legacy_assess) ──────────────────────────────┐
 │                                                                │
 │    R_GET_GCTA_INTERVALS ──► processed mapping + QTL regions    │
 │    R_ASSESS_SIMS ──────────► *_mapping.tsv                     │
 │              ▼                                                 │
 │    simulation_assessment_results.tsv                            │
 └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Both paths expand each upstream GWA result by threshold — every (mode, type) combination is analyzed once for BF and once for EIGEN — doubling the output relative to Phase 3.
For a comparison of the two paths, see the Assessment Cross-Validation documentation.

title: Phase 4: QTL Detection & Assessment
text:
Shared Concepts
Both paths implement the same logical algorithm for QTL interval detection. The key concepts are described here once and referenced in the path-specific sections below.

Significance Thresholds
A significance threshold converts raw p-values into a binary significant/not significant call for each marker. The pipeline supports three methods:








Method
Formula
Source




BF (Bonferroni)
-log10(alpha / n_markers)
Total marker count from the marker set


EIGEN
-log10(alpha / n_independent_tests)
Eigenvalue decomposition of the genotype matrix


User-defined
Numeric value used directly
Passed via --sthresh parameter



Where alpha defaults to 0.05. The threshold is applied on the -log10(p) scale.
Source: R/analysis.R::calculate_threshold()


QTL Interval Detection Algorithm
Both paths apply the same four-step procedure to flag significant markers, group them into QTL intervals, and define confidence intervals:

Flag significant markers — compute -log10(p) for each marker and flag those where log10p >= threshold as significant (significant = 1 in DB path, aboveBF = 1 in legacy path)
Proportion check — if more than 15% of markers are significant, the threshold is considered too lenient and QTL intervals are not defined (all interval columns set to NA)
Group significant markers by proximity — significant markers are assigned sequential peak_id values. Two significant markers on the same chromosome within group_qtl marker-index distance are assigned to the same peak; otherwise a new peak ID is assigned
Define confidence intervals — for each peak group, the CI boundary is set to ci_size markers beyond the outermost significant marker in the group, clamped to chromosome bounds. The peak marker is the one with the highest -log10(p) in the group

Parameters:








Parameter
Default
Description




--group_qtl
1000
Marker-index distance for grouping significant markers into the same QTL


--ci_size
150
Number of markers flanking the peak group to define the CI boundary


--alpha
0.05
Significance level for threshold calculation



Source (DB path): R/analysis.R::analyze_mapping() which calls flag_significant_markers() → check_significant_proportion() → group_markers_to_intervals() → define_confidence_intervals()
Source (legacy path): bin/Get_GCTA_Intervals.R::process_mapping_df() which implements the same logic inline


Assessment Classification
After QTL intervals are defined, each QTL (simulated or detected) is classified into one of four outcome categories:










Designation
Simulated
Detected
Significant
Meaning




Detected.CV
TRUE
TRUE
TRUE
True positive — causal variant within a detected interval, peak is significant


Missed.CV
TRUE
FALSE
FALSE
False negative — causal variant not within any detected interval


CV.Not.Significant.In.Interval
TRUE
TRUE
FALSE
Causal variant within a detected interval, but its own marker is not significant


False.Discovery
FALSE
TRUE
TRUE
False positive — detected interval contains no causal variant



Source: R/sim_performance.R::designate_qtl()

title: Phase 4: QTL Detection & Assessment
text:
Stage 1: Database Ingestion (DB Path Only)
The DB path writes marker sets and raw GWA results to a Hive-partitioned Parquet database before performing QTL analysis. This enables DuckDB-based queries at analysis time and decouples threshold application from data storage.
Three processes run sequentially with two barrier points:

1. DB_MIGRATION_WRITE_MARKER_SET
Source: modules/db_migration/write_marker_set/main.nf  •  Script: resources/usr/bin/write_marker_set.R
Writes the LD-pruned marker set and EIGEN independent tests count to the Parquet database. One invocation per (group, maf) combination.
Command:
write_marker_set.R --group ${group} --maf ${maf} --bim ${bim_file} \
    --eigen ${n_indep_tests} --base_dir ${base_dir}







Flag
Description




--group
Population/strain group identifier


--maf
MAF threshold used for this marker set


--bim
Path to PLINK .bim file from PLINK_RECODE_VCF


--eigen
Path to independent tests file from LOCAL_COMPILE_EIGENS


--base_dir
Database output directory



R functions called:








Function
Source
Purpose




read_bim_file()
R/io.R
Read PLINK .bim file into a marker dataframe


read_eigen_file()
R/io.R
Read single-value EIGEN independent tests file


write_marker_set()
R/database.R
Write marker Parquet file and metadata



Barrier role: All marker sets must complete before DB_MIGRATION_WRITE_GWA_TO_DB proceeds. The pipeline collects all done signals and validates that at least one marker set was written.
Inputs / Outputs:








Direction
File
Description




Input
TO_SIMS.bim
PLINK binary marker information file


Input
*_total_independent_tests.txt
EIGEN independent tests count


Output
{base_dir}/marker_sets/*.parquet
Marker set Parquet file


Output
{base_dir}/marker_sets/marker_set_metadata.parquet
Marker set metadata





2. DB_MIGRATION_WRITE_GWA_TO_DB
Source: modules/db_migration/write_gwa_to_db/main.nf  •  Script: resources/usr/bin/write_gwa_to_db.R
Writes raw GWA output to Hive-partitioned Parquet. Runs once per (mode, type) combination, not per threshold — thresholds are applied at query time during DB_MIGRATION_ANALYZE_QTL.
Command:
write_gwa_to_db.R \
    --group ${group} --maf ${maf} --nqtl ${nqtl} --effect ${effect} \
    --rep ${rep} --h2 ${h2} --mode ${mode} --type ${type} \
    --gwa_file ${gwa_file} --base_dir ${base_dir}



Flag
Description




--group
Population/strain group identifier


--maf
MAF threshold


--nqtl
Number of simulated QTLs


--effect
Effect size distribution (e.g. gamma, 0.2-0.3)


--rep
Simulation replicate number


--h2
Heritability


--mode
GWA mode (inbred or loco)


--type
PCA type (pca or nopca)


--gwa_file
Path to GCTA output file (.fastGWA or .mlma)


--base_dir
Database output directory



R functions called:








Function
Source
Purpose




read_raw_gwa_file()
R/io.R
Auto-detect and read .fastGWA or .mlma format


generate_mapping_id()
R/database.R
Construct unique mapping ID from parameters


write_mapping_partitioned()
R/database.R
Write to Hive-partitioned Parquet structure



LOCO deduplication: LOCO .mlma files may contain duplicate marker rows. The script applies distinct(marker, .keep_all = TRUE) before writing.
Algorithm mapping: The mode/type Nextflow values are translated to database fields:

inbred → LMM-EXACT-INBRED, loco → LMM-EXACT-LOCO
pca → pca = TRUE, nopca → pca = FALSE

Inputs / Outputs:








Direction
File
Description




Input
*.fastGWA or *.mlma
Raw GCTA GWA output from GCTA_PERFORM_GWA


Output
{base_dir}/mappings/population={pop}/mapping_id={id}/data.parquet
Hive-partitioned mapping data





3. DB_MIGRATION_AGGREGATE_METADATA
Source: modules/db_migration/aggregate_metadata/main.nf  •  Script: resources/usr/bin/aggregate_metadata.R
Scans all mapping Parquet files and builds a metadata summary table. Runs once after all DB_MIGRATION_WRITE_GWA_TO_DB processes complete.
Command:
aggregate_metadata.R ${base_dir}
The script reads the first row of each Parquet file to extract metadata columns (mapping_id, population, maf, nqtl, rep, h2, effect, algorithm, pca, trait) and counts total markers per file.
Barrier role: The database must be fully populated before analysis queries begin. The summary output gates all downstream DB_MIGRATION_ANALYZE_QTL invocations.
Inputs / Outputs:








Direction
File
Description




Input
{base_dir}/mappings/**/data.parquet
All mapping Parquet files


Output
{base_dir}/mappings_metadata.parquet
Aggregated metadata table


Output
aggregation_summary.txt
Human-readable summary (barrier signal)





Database Structure
The Parquet database uses a Hive partitioning scheme:
{outputDir}/db/
 ├── marker_sets/
 │   ├── {population}_{maf}_markers.parquet
 │   └── marker_set_metadata.parquet
 ├── mappings/
 │   └── population={pop}/
 │       └── mapping_id={id}/
 │           └── data.parquet
 └── mappings_metadata.parquet
At query time, DuckDB reads the partitioned Parquet files directly via the read_parquet() function with hive_partitioning = true. This allows efficient filtering by population and mapping ID without loading all data into memory. See R/queries.R::open_mapping_db() for the connection setup.

title: Phase 4: QTL Detection & Assessment
text:
Stage 2: QTL Detection

DB Path: DB_MIGRATION_ANALYZE_QTL
Source: modules/db_migration/analyze_qtl/main.nf  •  Script: resources/usr/bin/analyze_qtl.R
Queries the populated database, applies the significance threshold, detects QTL intervals, and writes a QTL regions TSV. Runs once per upstream parameter combination × threshold (BF and EIGEN), doubling the cardinality relative to Phase 3.
Command:
analyze_qtl.R \
    --group ${group} --maf ${maf} --nqtl ${nqtl} --effect ${effect} \
    --rep ${rep} --h2 ${h2} --mode ${mode} --type ${type} \
    --threshold ${threshold} \
    --base_dir ${base_dir} --ci_size ${ci_size} --snp_grouping ${snp_grouping} \
    --alpha ${alpha}







Flag
Description




--threshold
Threshold method: BF or EIGEN


--base_dir
Database root directory


--ci_size
Number of flanking markers for CI boundary


--snp_grouping
Marker-index distance for grouping significant markers


--alpha
Significance level

title: Phase 3: GWAS Mapping
text:
The third phase of the pipeline maps simulated phenotypes using GCTA to evaluate GWA power and precision. It takes the PLINK filesets from Phase 2 and produces per-marker association statistics under four mapping conditions.
The processing flow is:
PLINK binaries (from Phase 2)
 │
 ├─ GCTA_MAKE_GRM ──────────► GRM binaries + check_vp.hsq
 │                                   │
 ▼                                   ▼
 PYTHON_CHECK_VP ────────────► variance-checked .pheno file
 │
 ▼
 GCTA_PERFORM_GWA ───────────► .fastGWA or .mlma association results
                                (to Phase 4: QTL Detection & Assessment)
Each process is described below with the exact commands run, key parameters, and links to external tool documentation.
Mode and type parameterization: The mapping phase is parameterized by two variables defined in main.nf:

mode: "inbred" or "loco" — determines the type of GRM and the GWA algorithm
type: "pca" or "nopca" — determines whether the first principal component eigenvector is included as a covariate

ch_mode = Channel.of(
    ["inbred", "fastGWA"],
    ["loco", "mlma"]
)

ch_type = Channel.of(
    "pca",
    "nopca"
)
Each simulation replicate is mapped under all four combinations (inbred x pca, inbred x nopca, loco x pca, loco x nopca), driven by Nextflow channel combine operations. The mode crossing happens at GCTA_MAKE_GRM and the type crossing happens at GCTA_PERFORM_GWA.

title: Phase 3: GWAS Mapping
text:
1. GCTA_MAKE_GRM
Source: modules/gcta/make_grm/main.nf
Builds a genetic relatedness matrix (GRM) and estimates phenotypic variance via REML. This process runs two sequential GCTA commands.

Step 1: Build the GRM
The GRM construction method depends on the mode parameter:
if [[ ${mode} == "inbred" ]]; then
    GRM_OPTION="--make-grm-inbred"
else
    GRM_OPTION="--make-grm"
fi

gcta64 --bfile TO_SIMS_${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group} \
        --autosome --maf ${maf} ${GRM_OPTION} \
        --out TO_SIMS_${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_gcta_grm_${mode} \
        --thread-num ${task.cpus}



Flag
Purpose
Docs




--make-grm-inbred
Construct a kinship matrix for inbred organisms, adjusting for reduced heterozygosity
GCTA GRM


--make-grm
Construct a standard genome-wide relatedness matrix
GCTA GRM


--autosome
Use only autosomal markers
GCTA GRM


--maf
Filter markers below the minor allele frequency threshold
GCTA GRM




mode == "inbred": Uses --make-grm-inbred, which constructs a kinship matrix tailored for populations of inbred organisms. The inbred GRM adjusts relatedness estimates to account for the reduced heterozygosity characteristic of inbred lines.
mode == "loco": Uses --make-grm, which constructs a standard genome-wide relatedness matrix using all autosomal markers.

Because main.nf defines ch_mode with both ["inbred", "fastGWA"] and ["loco", "mlma"], each simulation replicate produces two GRMs — one inbred and one standard.


Step 2: REML variance estimation
gcta64 --grm TO_SIMS_${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_gcta_grm_${mode} \
        --pheno ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_sims.phen \
        --reml --out check_vp \
        --thread-num ${task.cpus}
This estimates the phenotypic variance (Vp) from the simulated phenotype data using restricted maximum likelihood (REML). See GCTA GREML analysis documentation.



Flag
Purpose




--grm
Input GRM prefix from step 1


--pheno
Simulated phenotype file


--reml
Perform REML analysis to estimate variance components

title: Phase 3: GWAS Mapping
text:
2. PYTHON_CHECK_VP
Source: modules/python/check_vp.nf
Inspects the estimated phenotypic variance (Vp) from the check_vp.hsq file produced by GCTA REML. This step ensures that simulated phenotypes exhibit sufficient variance, preventing near-zero variance from causing numerical issues in the association mapping step.
Command:
python3 check_vp.py --check_vp ${hsq_in} --simulated_phenos tmp.pheno
mv new_phenos.temp ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_sims.pheno

Script: bin/check_vp.py







Function
Description




get_vp()
Reads the check_vp.hsq file and extracts the Vp value from the row where Source == “Vp”


increase_pheno_var()
Multiplies all phenotype values (column 3) by 1000


read_original_phenos()
Reads the phenotype file unchanged


write_phenos_to_temp()
Writes output to new_phenos.temp





Variance check logic

Parse check_vp.hsq to extract the Vp value
If Vp < 0.000001: multiply all phenotype values by 1000 to increase variance
If Vp >= 0.000001: retain original phenotype values without modification
Write output to new_phenos.temp, then rename to the final *_sims.pheno filename

If the .hsq file is missing or Vp cannot be parsed, the script falls back to using the original phenotype values.


Example .pheno output
From test data (data/test_data/.../PYTHON_CHECK_VP/5_1_0.2_0.05_gamma_ce.96.allout15_irrepressible.grosbeak_sims.pheno):
MY2713 MY2713 -6.04369
JU323 JU323 11.1615
XZ1734 XZ1734 4.15316
QG4080 QG4080 9.589
In this case Vp was well above the threshold, so phenotype values are unchanged from the GCTA simulation output.

title: Phase 3: GWAS Mapping
text:
3. GCTA_PERFORM_GWA
Source: modules/gcta/perform_gwa/main.nf
Performs genome-wide association mapping. This process receives the GRM and variance-checked phenotype data, then executes three sub-steps controlled by the mode and type parameters.

Step 1: Create sparse GRM
This step always runs regardless of mode or type:
gcta64 --grm TO_SIMS_${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_gcta_grm_${mode} \
    --make-bK-sparse ${sparse_cut} \
    --out ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_sparse_grm_${mode} \
    --thread-num ${task.cpus}



Flag
Purpose
Docs




--make-bK-sparse
Set all off-diagonal GRM values with absolute value ≤ sparse_cut (default: 0.05) to zero
GCTA GRM



This produces a sparse representation of the GRM, reducing memory usage for the association mapping step.


Step 2: Conditional PCA
The type parameter controls whether PCA eigenvectors are extracted as covariates:
if [[ ${type} == "pca" ]]; then
    gcta64 --grm TO_SIMS_${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_gcta_grm_${mode} \
        --pca 1 \
        --out ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_sparse_grm_${mode} \
        --thread-num ${task.cpus}

    COVAR="--qcovar ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_sparse_grm_${mode}.eigenvec"
else
    COVAR=""
fi

type == "pca": Extracts the first principal component from the GRM (--pca 1), producing an .eigenvec file. This eigenvector is passed as a quantitative covariate (--qcovar) to the mapping command to control for population structure.
type == "nopca": No PCA is performed. The COVAR variable is set to an empty string, so no covariate is included in the mapping command.



Step 3: Association mapping
The mode parameter determines both the GCTA mapping algorithm and the GRM input flag:
if [[ ${mode} == "inbred" ]]; then
    GRM_OPTION='--grm-sparse'
    COMMAND='--fastGWA-mlm-exact'
else
    GRM_OPTION="--grm"
    COMMAND="--mlma-loco"
fi

gcta64 ${COMMAND} \
    --bfile TO_SIMS_${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group} \
    ${GRM_OPTION} ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_sparse_grm_${mode} \
    ${COVAR} \
    --out ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_lmm-exact_${mode}_${type} \
    --pheno ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_sims.pheno \
    --maf ${maf} \
    --thread-num ${task.cpus}
Inbred mode (mode == "inbred"):

Uses --fastGWA-mlm-exact to perform an exact mixed linear model (MLM) association analysis (GCTA fastGWA docs)
Uses --grm-sparse to input the sparse GRM created from the inbred relatedness matrix
Output format: .fastGWA (columns: CHR, SNP, POS, A1, A2, N, AF1, BETA, SE, P)

LOCO mode (mode == "loco"):

Uses --mlma-loco to perform MLM-based association using a leave-one-chromosome-out approach, where the GRM excludes the chromosome of the tested variant (GCTA MLMA docs)
Uses --grm to input the sparse GRM created from the standard relatedness matrix
Output format: .loco.mlma (columns: Chr, SNP, bp, A1, A2, Freq, b, se, p), renamed to .mlma after completion

if [[ ${mode} == "loco" ]]; then
    mv "${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_lmm-exact_${mode}_${type}.loco.mlma" \
       "${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_lmm-exact_${mode}_${type}.mlma"
fi

title: Phase 2: Trait Simulation
text:
The second phase of the pipeline transforms the LD-pruned PLINK binary filesets (from Phase 1) into simulated phenotype data with known causal variants. These outputs feed into GWAS mapping (Phase 3).
The processing flow is:
PLINK binaries (from Phase 1)
 ├─ PYTHON_SIMULATE_EFFECTS_GLOBAL ──► causal_variants.txt
 │                                        │
 ▼                                        ▼
 GCTA_SIMULATE_PHENOTYPES ──────────► .phen + .par files
 │
 ▼
 PLINK_UPDATE_BY_H2 ──────────► Phenotype-annotated PLINK binaries
                                 (to Phase 3: GWAS Mapping)
Each process is described below with the exact commands run, key parameters, and links to external tool documentation.
Cartesian product expansion: The each qualifiers on rep, nqtl, effect, and h2 in the Nextflow process definitions create all parameter combinations. Every PLINK fileset from Phase 1 is crossed with every combination of replicate number, number of QTL, effect size distribution, and heritability value, so downstream processes run independently for each point in the parameter grid.

title: Phase 2: Trait Simulation
text:
The second phase of the pipeline transforms the LD-pruned PLINK binary filesets (from Phase 1) into simulated phenotype data with known causal variants. These outputs feed into GWAS mapping (Phase 3).
The processing flow is:
PLINK binaries (from Phase 1)
 ├─ PYTHON_SIMULATE_EFFECTS_GLOBAL ──► causal_variants.txt
 │                                        │
 ▼                                        ▼
 GCTA_SIMULATE_PHENOTYPES ──────────► .phen + .par files
 │
 ▼
 PLINK_UPDATE_BY_H2 ──────────► Phenotype-annotated PLINK binaries
                                 (to Phase 3: GWAS Mapping)
Each process is described below with the exact commands run, key parameters, and links to external tool documentation.
Cartesian product expansion: The each qualifiers on rep, nqtl, effect, and h2 in the Nextflow process definitions create all parameter combinations. Every PLINK fileset from Phase 1 is crossed with every combination of replicate number, number of QTL, effect size distribution, and heritability value, so downstream processes run independently for each point in the parameter grid.

title: Phase 2: Trait Simulation
text:
1. PYTHON_SIMULATE_EFFECTS_GLOBAL
Source: modules/python/simulate_effects_global/main.nf
Selects random causal variants from the LD-pruned marker set and assigns effect sizes. This process executes the bin/create_causal_vars.py script.
Command:
python create_causal_vars.py TO_SIMS.bim ${nqtl} ${effect}
mv causal_vars.txt causal.variants.sim.${nqtl}.${rep}.txt

Script functions







Function
Description




load_strain_set_variants()
Reads marker IDs from column 2 of the .bim file


select_variants()
Random sample of nQTL markers without replacement via np.random.choice


simulate_effect_gamma()
Gamma(shape=0.4, scale=1.66) distribution with random sign (+1 or -1)


simulate_effect_uniform()
Uniform(low, high) distribution with random sign (+1 or -1)





Effect type selection
The third command-line argument determines which effect size distribution is used:

gamma keyword — calls simulate_effect_gamma() with default shape=0.4 and scale=1.66
low-high range string (e.g. 0.4-0.9) — calls simulate_effect_uniform() with the parsed low and high bounds



Inputs / Outputs








Direction
File
Description




Input
TO_SIMS.bim
PLINK marker information file from Phase 1 (variant IDs in column 2)


Input
nqtl (val)
Number of causal variants to select


Input
effect (val)
Effect size specification: gamma or low-high range


Input
rep (val)
Replicate number (used only in output filename)


Output
causal.variants.sim.${nqtl}.${rep}.txt
Space-separated file: marker ID (col 1) and effect size (col 2), one row per causal variant





Example output
From test data (data/test_data/.../PYTHON_SIMULATE_EFFECTS_GLOBAL/causal.variants.sim.5.1.txt) with nQTL=5 and effect=gamma:
2:14378543 0.38901223435837223
2:7459092 -4.532985639400811
3:1537674 -0.0063686130105886415
4:16212978 0.47805937866664927
2:13733845 -0.03046281285149475
Each line contains a marker ID in CHROM:POS format and its assigned effect size (sign indicates direction).

title: Phase 2: Trait Simulation
text:
2. GCTA_SIMULATE_PHENOTYPES
Source: modules/gcta/simulate_phenotypes/main.nf
Uses GCTA’s GWAS Simulation functionality to simulate quantitative phenotypes from the causal variants selected in step 1.
Command:
gcta64 --bfile TO_SIMS \
     --simu-qt \
     --simu-causal-loci ${causal_variants} \
     --simu-hsq ${h2} \
     --simu-rep 1 \
     --autosome-num 6 \
     --thread-num ${task.cpus} \
     --out ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_sims







Flag
Purpose




--simu-qt
Simulate quantitative traits


--simu-causal-loci
Causal variants file from step 1 (marker IDs and effect sizes)


--simu-hsq
Target heritability (h2); from the --h2 pipeline parameter, crossed in via each h2


--simu-rep 1
Single replicate per GCTA call (pipeline-level --reps parameter controls repetition via Nextflow each)


--autosome-num 6
C. elegans has 6 chromosomes (5 autosomes + X)


--thread-num
Number of threads




Inputs / Outputs








Direction
File
Description




Input
TO_SIMS.bed/.bim/.fam
PLINK binary fileset from Phase 1


Input
causal.variants.sim.${nqtl}.${rep}.txt
Causal variants file from step 1


Input
h2 (val)
Target heritability (crossed in via each h2)


Output
*_sims.phen
Simulated phenotype values (FID, IID, phenotype)


Output
*_sims.par
Simulation parameters (QTL, RefAllele, Frequency, Effect)





Example .par output
From test data (data/test_data/.../GCTA_SIMULATE_PHENOTYPES/5_1_0.2_0.05_gamma_ce.96.allout15_irrepressible.grosbeak_sims.par):
QTL RefAllele   Frequency   Effect
2:7459092   A   0.0520833   -4.53299
2:13733845  A   0.125   -0.0304628
2:14378543  A   0.104167    0.389012
3:1537674   G   0.125   -0.00636861
4:16212978  T   0.0625  0.478059
The .par file has a header and four columns:

QTL — marker ID in CHROM:POS format
RefAllele — reference allele at the causal locus
Frequency — allele frequency in the strain set
Effect — effect size used in the simulation



Example .phen output
First 10 lines from 5_1_0.2_0.05_gamma_ce.96.allout15_irrepressible.grosbeak_sims.phen:
MY2713 MY2713 -6.04369
JU323 JU323 11.1615
XZ1734 XZ1734 4.15316
QG4080 QG4080 9.589
DL226 DL226 -4.79251
ECA2533 ECA2533 8.34439
XZ1672 XZ1672 5.00596
ED3046 ED3046 -2.37417
NIC1786 NIC1786 11.8277
NIC274 NIC274 -22.5104
The .phen file has no header and three space-separated columns:

Column 1 — Family ID (FID)
Column 2 — Individual ID (IID)
Column 3 — Simulated phenotype value

title: Phase 2: Trait Simulation
text:
3. PLINK_UPDATE_BY_H2
Source: modules/plink/update_by_h2/main.nf
Integrates the simulated phenotype values into the PLINK binary fileset and renames the output files to include all simulation parameters. This produces the phenotype-annotated PLINK files used by Phase 3 (GWAS Mapping).
Command:
plink --bfile TO_SIMS \
    --make-bed \
    --snps-only \
    --biallelic-only \
    --maf ${maf} \
    --set-missing-var-ids @:# \
    --geno \
    --recode \
    --out TO_SIMS_${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group} \
    --allow-extra-chr \
    --allow-no-sex \
    --pheno ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_sims.pheno



Flag
Purpose
Docs




--make-bed
Create PLINK binary fileset (.bed, .bim, .fam)
plink data


--snps-only
Exclude indels, retain only SNPs
plink filter


--biallelic-only
Exclude multiallelic sites
plink filter


--maf
Remove variants below the minor allele frequency threshold
plink filter


--set-missing-var-ids @:#
Assign IDs to variants missing an rsID, using CHROM:POS format
plink data


--geno
Exclude variants with missing genotype rates above the default threshold (0.1)
plink filter


--recode
Also produce text-format .ped/.map files
plink data


--allow-extra-chr
Permit non-standard chromosome codes
plink filter


--allow-no-sex
Handle samples with missing sex information
plink filter


--pheno
Embed simulated phenotype values into the .fam file
plink assoc









Note.phen to .pheno file rename



The GCTA simulation step outputs a .phen file, but the PLINK process input declaration expects a .pheno extension: ${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}_sims.pheno. Nextflow’s file staging handles this rename automatically when the output from GCTA_SIMULATE_PHENOTYPES is wired into PLINK_UPDATE_BY_H2.








NoteRe-applied filtering flags



The filtering flags (--snps-only, --biallelic-only, --maf, --set-missing-var-ids, --geno) are the same as those used in Phase 1’s PLINK_RECODE_VCF. They are re-applied here for consistency across all PLINK commands in the pipeline, even though the input data has already been filtered.



Inputs / Outputs








Direction
File
Description




Input
TO_SIMS.bed/.bim/.fam
PLINK binary fileset from Phase 1


Input
*_sims.pheno
Simulated phenotype file (renamed from .phen)


Input
*_sims.par
Simulation parameter file (passed through)


Output
TO_SIMS_${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}.bed/.bim/.fam
Phenotype-annotated PLINK binary fileset


Output
TO_SIMS_${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}.map/.ped
PLINK text-format fileset


Output
TO_SIMS_${nqtl}_${rep}_${h2}_${maf}_${effect}_${group}.nosex/.log
PLINK auxiliary outputs


Output
*_sims.pheno + *_sims.par
Phenotype and parameter files (passed through)





Output naming convention
Output files follow the pattern TO_SIMS_{nqtl}_{rep}_{h2}_{maf}_{effect}_{group}, encoding all simulation parameters in the filename:

nqtl — number of simulated QTL
rep — replicate number
h2 — target heritability
maf — minor allele frequency threshold
effect — effect size distribution (gamma or range)
group — strain set name

title: Database Structure
text:
The DB path stores pipeline outputs in a Parquet database queried at analysis time via DuckDB. The database contains two primary data domains:

Marker sets — static variant data from LD-pruned PLINK files, one file per (population, MAF) combination
Mappings — per-marker association statistics from GCTA GWAS output, Hive-partitioned by population and mapping ID

Two metadata tables provide summary lookups without scanning the full data files.
{outputDir}/db/
 ├── marker_sets/
 │   ├── {population}_{maf}_markers.parquet      ◄── marker data
 │   └── marker_set_metadata.parquet              ◄── EIGEN + marker counts
 ├── mappings/
 │   └── population={pop}/
 │       └── mapping_id={id}/
 │           └── data.parquet                     ◄── GWA statistics
 └── mappings_metadata.parquet                    ◄── mapping summary table
All Parquet files use Snappy compression. Schemas are defined as Arrow schema objects in R/database.R.

title: Database Structure
text:
The DB path stores pipeline outputs in a Parquet database queried at analysis time via DuckDB. The database contains two primary data domains:

Marker sets — static variant data from LD-pruned PLINK files, one file per (population, MAF) combination
Mappings — per-marker association statistics from GCTA GWAS output, Hive-partitioned by population and mapping ID

Two metadata tables provide summary lookups without scanning the full data files.
{outputDir}/db/
 ├── marker_sets/
 │   ├── {population}_{maf}_markers.parquet      ◄── marker data
 │   └── marker_set_metadata.parquet              ◄── EIGEN + marker counts
 ├── mappings/
 │   └── population={pop}/
 │       └── mapping_id={id}/
 │           └── data.parquet                     ◄── GWA statistics
 └── mappings_metadata.parquet                    ◄── mapping summary table
All Parquet files use Snappy compression. Schemas are defined as Arrow schema objects in R/database.R.

title: Database Structure
text:
Marker Sets
A marker set represents the LD-pruned variant panel for a given population and MAF threshold. Marker sets are written once during database ingestion and queried repeatedly during QTL analysis.

Data Source
Marker data originates from two upstream processes:







Source
Columns provided




PLINK .bim file (PLINK_RECODE_VCF)
marker, CHROM, POS, A1, A2


EIGEN independent tests file (LOCAL_COMPILE_EIGENS)
n_independent_tests (metadata only)



The .bim file does not contain allele frequencies, so AF1 is set to NA in the marker set. Allele frequencies are available per-mapping from the raw GWA output and are resolved at query time via COALESCE(m.AF1, mk.AF1).
Source: R/io.R::read_bim_file()


Markers Schema
File: {base_dir}/marker_sets/{population}_{maf}_markers.parquet








Column
Arrow Type
Description




marker
utf8
Variant identifier in CHROM:POS format (e.g. 1:157580)


CHROM
utf8
Chromosome (numeric string: "1", "2", …, "6")


POS
int32
Genomic position (bp)


A1
utf8
Allele 1 (minor allele from PLINK)


A2
utf8
Allele 2 (major allele from PLINK)


AF1
float64
Allele frequency of A1 (NA when sourced from .bim)


population
utf8
Population/strain set identifier (e.g. "complete")


maf
float64
MAF threshold used to generate this marker set



Key: (population, maf) uniquely identifies a marker set. Within a marker set, marker (equivalently CHROM + POS) is unique.
Source: R/database.R::markers_schema(), R/database.R::write_marker_set()


Marker Set Metadata Schema
File: {base_dir}/marker_sets/marker_set_metadata.parquet








Column
Arrow Type
Description




population
utf8
Population/strain set identifier


maf
float64
MAF threshold


n_markers
int32
Total number of markers in this set


n_independent_tests
float64
Number of independent tests from EIGEN decomposition


eigen_source_file
utf8
Filename of the EIGEN independent tests file


created_at
timestamp[us]
Timestamp when this record was written



Key: (population, maf) uniquely identifies a record.
This table is used by get_threshold_params() to retrieve marker counts and EIGEN values for significance threshold calculation without reading the full marker Parquet files.
Source: R/database.R::marker_set_metadata_schema(), R/database.R::write_marker_set_metadata()


Example: Querying a Marker Set
# Via R helper function
markers <- read_marker_set("complete", 0.05, base_dir = "results/db")

# Via DuckDB (used internally by open_mapping_db)
con <- DBI::dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb(), ":memory:")
DBI::dbExecute(con, "
  CREATE VIEW markers AS
  SELECT * FROM read_parquet('results/db/marker_sets/*_markers.parquet')
")
result <- DBI::dbGetQuery(con, "
  SELECT COUNT(*) as n_markers, MIN(POS) as min_pos, MAX(POS) as max_pos
  FROM markers
  WHERE population = 'complete' AND maf = 0.05
  GROUP BY CHROM
  ORDER BY CHROM
")

title: Database Structure
text:
Mappings
A mapping stores the per-marker association statistics from a single GWAS run. Each mapping corresponds to one combination of simulation parameters and mapping method. Mappings are written during database ingestion and queried during QTL analysis and assessment.

Data Source
Mapping data originates from GCTA GWA output files:








GWA Mode
File Format
Columns




inbred (fastGWA-mlm-exact)
.fastGWA
CHR, SNP, POS, A1, A2, N, AF1, BETA, SE, P


loco (mlma-loco)
.mlma
Chr, SNP, bp, A1, A2, Freq, b, se, p



Both formats are normalized to a common schema by read_raw_gwa_file(). Key normalizations:

Column names standardized (e.g. Chr → CHROM, bp → POS, Freq → AF1)
marker column created from SNP column (already in CHROM:POS format)
N column dropped (absent from .mlma, unused downstream)
log10p not stored — computed at query time via safe_log10p(P) in R/analysis.R

LOCO deduplication: .mlma files from LOCO mapping may contain duplicate marker rows. These are deduplicated by distinct(marker, .keep_all = TRUE) before writing.
Source: R/io.R::read_raw_gwa_file()


Mappings Schema
File: {base_dir}/mappings/population={pop}/mapping_id={id}/data.parquet








Column
Arrow Type
Description




marker
utf8
Variant identifier in CHROM:POS format


mapping_id
utf8
Unique mapping identifier (see format below)


AF1
float64
Allele frequency from GWA output


BETA
float64
Effect size estimate


SE
float64
Standard error of effect estimate


P
float64
P-value from association test


var.exp
float64
Variance explained (NA for the inline DB path)


nqtl
int32
Number of simulated causal QTLs


rep
int32
Simulation replicate number


h2
float64
Simulated heritability


effect
utf8
Effect size distribution (e.g. "gamma", "0.2-0.3")


algorithm
utf8
Mapping algorithm (see below)


pca
boolean
Whether PCA covariate was included


population
utf8
Population/strain set identifier


maf
float64
MAF threshold


trait
utf8
Trait identifier ("{nqtl}_{rep}_{h2}")



Source: R/database.R::mappings_schema(), R/database.R::write_mapping_partitioned()


Mapping ID Format
The mapping_id is a deterministic string constructed from simulation and mapping parameters:
{nqtl}_{rep}_{h2}_{maf}_{effect}_{population}_{algorithm}_{PCA|noPCA}
Examples:

5_1_0.2_0.05_gamma_complete_LMM-EXACT-INBRED_PCA
2_3_0.8_0.05_0.2-0.3_complete_LMM-EXACT-LOCO_noPCA

Source: R/database.R::generate_mapping_id()


Algorithm Values
The algorithm column encodes the GCTA mapping method:



Nextflow mode
Database algorithm
GCTA Method




inbred
LMM-EXACT-INBRED
--fastGWA-mlm-exact


loco
LMM-EXACT-LOCO
--mlma-loco



The pca boolean tracks the Nextflow type parameter:



Nextflow type
Database pca




pca
TRUE


nopca
FALSE





AF1 Dual Storage
Allele frequency (AF1) is stored in both marker sets and mappings because:

Marker sets sourced from .bim files do not contain allele frequency (AF1 = NA)
Mappings sourced from GWA output files do contain allele frequency

At query time, the two are resolved with COALESCE(m.AF1, mk.AF1), preferring the mapping-level value. This is implemented in R/queries.R::query_for_threshold_analysis():
SELECT
  m.marker, m.P, m.BETA, m.SE,
  mk.CHROM, mk.POS,
  COALESCE(m.AF1, mk.AF1) AS AF1
FROM mappings m
LEFT JOIN markers mk
  ON m.marker = mk.marker
  AND m.population = mk.population
  AND m.maf = mk.maf
WHERE m.mapping_id = '{mapping_id}'


Hive Partitioning
Mappings use Hive-style directory partitioning:
mappings/
 └── population=complete/
     ├── mapping_id=5_1_0.2_0.05_gamma_complete_LMM-EXACT-INBRED_PCA/
     │   └── data.parquet
     ├── mapping_id=5_1_0.2_0.05_gamma_complete_LMM-EXACT-INBRED_noPCA/
     │   └── data.parquet
     └── mapping_id=5_1_0.2_0.05_gamma_complete_LMM-EXACT-LOCO_PCA/
         └── data.parquet
This structure enables:

True parallel writes — each Nextflow process writes to a unique directory with no file-level coordination
Partition pruning — DuckDB reads only the partitions matching a WHERE clause filter
Incremental population — new mappings can be added without rewriting existing files

DuckDB reads the partitioned structure with:
CREATE VIEW mappings AS
SELECT * FROM read_parquet(
  '{base_dir}/mappings/**/*.parquet',
  hive_partitioning = true,
  union_by_name = true
)
Source: R/database.R::write_mapping_partitioned(), R/queries.R::open_mapping_db()

title: Database Structure
text:
Mappings Metadata
The mappings metadata table provides a lightweight summary of all mappings in the database. It is built by DB_MIGRATION_AGGREGATE_METADATA after all GWA writes complete, enabling fast lookups without scanning the full Parquet files.

Mappings Metadata Schema
File: {base_dir}/mappings_metadata.parquet



Column
Arrow Type
Description




mapping_id
utf8
Unique mapping identifier


population
utf8
Population/strain set identifier


maf
float64
MAF threshold


nqtl
int32
Number of simulated causal QTLs


rep
int32
Simulation replicate number


h2
float64
Simulated heritability


effect
utf8
Effect size distribution


algorithm
utf8
Mapping algorithm


pca
boolean
Whether PCA covariate was included


trait
utf8
Trait identifier


n_markers
int32
Number of markers in this mapping


source_file
utf8
Original partition directory name


processed_at
timestamp[us]
When the metadata was aggregated


processing_version
utf8
Pipeline version string



Key: mapping_id uniquely identifies each record.
Source: R/database.R::metadata_schema(), modules/db_migration/aggregate_metadata/resources/usr/bin/aggregate_metadata.R

title: Database Structure
text:
Query Patterns
The database is accessed through DuckDB via helper functions in R/queries.R. All queries are read-only; writes go through R/database.R.

Connection Setup
open_mapping_db() creates an in-memory DuckDB connection and registers three views:








View
Source
Description




markers
marker_sets/*.parquet
All marker set files


mappings
mappings/**/*.parquet (Hive)
All mapping partitions


metadata
mappings_metadata.parquet
Mapping summary table


marker_set_metadata
marker_sets/marker_set_metadata.parquet
EIGEN values and marker counts





Key Query Functions








Function
Source
Purpose




query_for_threshold_analysis()
R/queries.R
Retrieve mapping data with marker coordinates joined — primary query for QTL detection


get_threshold_params()
R/database.R
Look up marker count and EIGEN independent tests for threshold calculation


query_by_mapping_id()
R/queries.R
Retrieve raw mapping data for a single mapping


query_mapping_data()
R/queries.R
Full mapping + marker join with optional filters


get_metadata()
R/queries.R
Read the mappings metadata summary table


list_populations()
R/queries.R
List available populations from partition directories


db_stats()
R/queries.R
Database-wide statistics (file counts, sizes, populations)



The query_for_threshold_analysis() function is the primary entry point used by analyze_qtl.R and assess_sims.R. It joins mappings with markers to resolve chromosome coordinates and allele frequencies, and validates CHROM:POS uniqueness per mapping.


Connection Caching
For batch operations, get_db_connection() provides a cached DuckDB connection that persists across multiple queries. The cache is keyed by base_dir and automatically reconnects if the connection becomes stale.
# Single query (creates and closes connection)
data <- query_for_threshold_analysis(mapping_id, base_dir)

# Batch operation (reuses cached connection)
con <- get_db_connection(base_dir, use_cache = TRUE)
for (id in mapping_ids) {
  data <- query_for_threshold_analysis(id, base_dir, con = con)
  # ... process data
}
close_cached_connection(base_dir)

title: Phase 1: Marker Set Generation
text:
The first phase of the pipeline transforms a multi-sample VCF into LD-pruned PLINK binary filesets and a numeric genotype matrix. These outputs feed into phenotype simulation (Phase 2) and GWAS mapping (Phase 3).
The processing flow is:
VCF
 ├─ LOCAL_GET_CONTIG_INFO ──► contig_mapping.tsv, contigs.txt
 │                                    │
 ▼                                    │
 BCFTOOLS_EXTRACT_STRAINS             │
 ▼                                    │
 BCFTOOLS_RENAME_CHROMS ◄─────────────┘
 ▼
 PLINK_RECODE_VCF ──────────► PLINK binaries (.bed/.bim/.fam), markers.txt
 │                                    │
 ▼                                    ▼
 (to Phase 2/3)          BCFTOOLS_CREATE_GENOTYPE_MATRIX ──► genotype matrix TSV
Each process is described below with the exact commands run, key parameters, and links to external tool documentation.

title: Phase 1: Marker Set Generation
text:
The first phase of the pipeline transforms a multi-sample VCF into LD-pruned PLINK binary filesets and a numeric genotype matrix. These outputs feed into phenotype simulation (Phase 2) and GWAS mapping (Phase 3).
The processing flow is:
VCF
 ├─ LOCAL_GET_CONTIG_INFO ──► contig_mapping.tsv, contigs.txt
 │                                    │
 ▼                                    │
 BCFTOOLS_EXTRACT_STRAINS             │
 ▼                                    │
 BCFTOOLS_RENAME_CHROMS ◄─────────────┘
 ▼
 PLINK_RECODE_VCF ──────────► PLINK binaries (.bed/.bim/.fam), markers.txt
 │                                    │
 ▼                                    ▼
 (to Phase 2/3)          BCFTOOLS_CREATE_GENOTYPE_MATRIX ──► genotype matrix TSV
Each process is described below with the exact commands run, key parameters, and links to external tool documentation.

title: Phase 1: Marker Set Generation
text:
LOCAL_GET_CONTIG_INFO (upstream helper)
Source: modules/local/get_contig_info/main.nf
Parses ##contig header lines from the VCF to build a name-to-numeric mapping (e.g. I → 1, II → 2, MtDNA → 7). This mapping is required because PLINK and GCTA expect numeric chromosome identifiers.
Commands:
# Extract ##contig headers and build tab-separated mapping (name → number)
zcat input.vcf.gz | head -n 200 | grep "##contig" | \
    awk 'BEGIN{COUNT=1}{split($1,A,"="); split(A[3],B,","); CHROM=B[1];
         printf "%s\t%i\n", CHROM, COUNT; COUNT=COUNT+1}' > contig_mapping.tsv

# Extract just the chromosome names
cut -f 1 contig_mapping.tsv > contigs.txt
Outputs:







File
Description




contig_mapping.tsv
Tab-separated file: chromosome name (col 1) and numeric ID (col 2)


contigs.txt
One chromosome name per line (used by main.nf for mito filtering and chromosome list)



Used by:

BCFTOOLS_RENAME_CHROMS — consumes contig_mapping.tsv as the rename mapping file
main.nf workflow logic — reads contigs.txt to identify the mitochondrial chromosome name and generate the list of autosomal chromosomes

title: Phase 1: Marker Set Generation
text:
1. BCFTOOLS_EXTRACT_STRAINS
Source: modules/bcftools/extract_strains/main.nf
Subsets the VCF to only the strains in the current strain set and removes any sites with missing genotypes. This produces a clean, strain-specific VCF for downstream processing.
Commands:
# Subset samples and filter missing sites, then index
bcftools view --threads ${task.cpus} -s ${strains} -O u ${vcf} | \
bcftools filter -i N_MISSING=0 -O z -o ${group}.vcf.gz
bcftools index --tbi ${group}.vcf.gz



Command
Purpose
Docs




bcftools view -s
Subset to specified sample names
bcftools view


bcftools filter -i N_MISSING=0
Keep only sites where no sample has a missing genotype
bcftools filter


bcftools index --tbi
Create a tabix (.tbi) index for the output VCF
bcftools index



Key flags:

-O u — pipe as uncompressed BCF (faster for piping between bcftools commands)
-O z — write bgzf-compressed VCF output

text:
2. BCFTOOLS_RENAME_CHROMS
Source: modules/bcftools/rename_chroms/main.nf
Renames chromosome identifiers from text (I, II, III, …) to numeric (1, 2, 3, …) using the mapping file produced by LOCAL_GET_CONTIG_INFO. Numeric chromosome names are required by PLINK and GCTA.
Commands:
# Stream VCF, rename chromosomes, compress and index
bcftools view --threads ${task.cpus} -O u ${vcf} | \
bcftools annotate --rename-chrs chr_mapping.tsv -O z -o ${id}_renamed.vcf.gz
bcftools index --tbi ${id}_renamed.vcf.gz



Command
Purpose
Docs

Stream VCF as uncompressed BCF for piping
bcftools view


bcftools annotate --rename-chrs
Rename chromosomes using a tab-separated mapping file
bcftools annotate


bcftools index --tbi
Create tabix index
bcftools index



Mapping file format: Tab-separated, one line per chromosome — old name in column 1, new name in column 2:
I       1
II      2

V       5
X       6
MtDNA   7

title: Phase 1: Marker Set Generation
text:
3. PLINK_RECODE_VCF
Source: modules/plink/recode_vcf/main.nf
Performs LD pruning and produces PLINK binary filesets for downstream GCTA analysis. This process runs two sequential PLINK commands: first to identify LD-pruned variants, then to create the binary files using only those variants.

Command 1: LD pruning
plink --vcf recoded.vcf.gz \
    --snps-only \
    --biallelic-only \
    --maf ${maf} \
    --set-missing-var-ids @:# \
    --indep-pairwise 50 10 0.8 \
    --geno \
    --not-chr ${mito_name} \
    --allow-extra-chr
This command computes pairwise LD and produces plink.prune.in (variant IDs to retain) and plink.prune.out (variant IDs to remove).



Flag
Purpose
Docs




--snps-only
Exclude indels, retain only SNPs
plink filter


--biallelic-only
Exclude multiallelic sites
plink filter


--maf ${maf}
Remove variants below the minor allele frequency threshold
plink filter


--set-missing-var-ids @:#
Assign IDs to variants missing an rsID, using CHROM:POS format
plink data


--indep-pairwise 50 10 0.8
Sliding-window LD pruning: window=50 variants, step=10, r2 threshold=0.8
plink LD


--geno
Exclude variants with missing genotype rates above the default threshold (0.1)
plink filter


--not-chr ${mito_name}
Exclude the mitochondrial chromosome
plink filter


--allow-extra-chr
Permit non-standard chromosome codes
plink filter





Command 2: Binary fileset creation
plink --vcf recoded.vcf.gz \
    --make-bed \
    --snps-only \
    --biallelic-only \
    --maf ${maf} \
    --set-missing-var-ids @:# \
    --extract plink.prune.in \
    --geno \
    --recode \
    --out TO_SIMS \
    --allow-extra-chr
Re-applies the same filtering flags for consistency and additionally:



Flag
Purpose
Docs




--make-bed
Create PLINK binary fileset (.bed, .bim, .fam)
plink data


--extract plink.prune.in
Retain only variants that passed LD pruning
plink data


--recode
Also produce text-format .ped/.map files
plink data


--out TO_SIMS
Set output file prefix
—





Post-processing: markers.txt
awk -F":" '$1=$1' OFS="\t" plink.prune.in | \
    sort -k1,1d -k2,2n > markers.txt
Converts the CHROM:POS variant IDs from plink.prune.in into a tab-separated CHROM  POS file, sorted by chromosome then position. This marker list is used by BCFTOOLS_CREATE_GENOTYPE_MATRIX to subset the VCF.

title: Phase 1: Marker Set Generation
text:
4. BCFTOOLS_CREATE_GENOTYPE_MATRIX
Source: modules/bcftools/create_genotype_matrix/main.nf
Creates a numeric genotype matrix (markers × strains) from the VCF, restricted to the LD-pruned marker set. Genotypes are encoded numerically for use in eigenvalue decomposition and downstream R analysis.
Commands:
# List and sort sample names
bcftools query -l ${vcf} | sort > sorted_samples.txt

# Extract genotypes for pruned markers, recode to numeric
bcftools view -v snps -S sorted_samples.txt -R ${markers} ${vcf} | \
    bcftools query --print-header -f '%CHROM\t%POS\t%REF\t%ALT[\t%GT]\n' | \
    sed 's/[[# 0-9]*]//g' | \   # clean header annotations
    sed 's/:GT//g' | \           # remove :GT suffix from sample names
    sed 's/0|0/-1/g' | \         # REF/REF homozygous → -1
    sed 's/1|1/1/g' | \          # ALT/ALT homozygous →  1
    sed 's/0|1/NA/g' | \         # heterozygous → NA
    sed 's/1|0/NA/g' | \         # heterozygous → NA
    sed 's/.|./NA/g' | \         # missing → NA
    sed 's/0\/0/-1/g' | \        # (unphased) REF/REF → -1
    sed 's/1\/1/1/g' | \         # (unphased) ALT/ALT →  1
    sed 's/0\/1/NA/g' | \        # (unphased) het → NA
    sed 's/1\/0/NA/g' | \        # (unphased) het → NA
    sed 's/.\/./NA/g' \          # (unphased) missing → NA
    > ${group}_${maf}_Genotype_Matrix.tsv



Command
Purpose
Docs




bcftools query -l
List sample names from the VCF header
bcftools query


bcftools view -v snps -S ... -R ...
Subset to SNPs, reorder samples, restrict to marker regions
bcftools view


bcftools query -f
Extract fields in a custom tab-separated format
bcftools query




Genotype encoding



Genotype
Encoding
Meaning




0\|0 or 0/0
-1
Homozygous reference


1\|1 or 1/1
1
Homozygous alternate


0\|1, 1\|0, 0/1, 1/0
NA
Heterozygous


.\|., ./.
NA
Missing



Design note: The encoding assumes inbred diploid organisms. C. elegans strains are expected to be homozygous, so heterozygous calls are treated as missing data (NA) rather than given an intermediate value.


Inputs / Outputs








Direction
File
Description




Input
recoded.vcf.gz + .tbi
Chromosome-renamed, strain-subset VCF


Input
markers.txt
LD-pruned marker positions from PLINK_RECODE_VCF


Output
${group}_${maf}_Genotype_Matrix.tsv
Tab-separated genotype matrix: rows are markers (CHROM, POS, REF, ALT), columns are strains
